title: The Four Locked Rooms
subtitle: Learn the method in three human script rooms, then clear Whale Cadence as the public gate.

doctrine.essays: The essays tie it all together.

claimStatus.note: Claim status: playable structural readings. The game teaches the method; essays carry the argument; Zenodo stamps the deposited record.

progression.lockedCopy: Solve the earlier room to open this door.
progression.bossLockedCopy: Whale knowledge is sealed until the three tutorial rooms are solved.
progression.bossUnlockedCopy: Public gate unlocked: words have run out, so cadence has to carry the signal.
progression.gateCopy: Whale is the public gate now. No gold pulse; use what rooms I-III taught you.

rooms.name: Calendar Room
rooms.headline: Calendar or coincidence?
rooms.lede: A damaged tablet repeats. Is there a real calendar hiding here, or are we fooling ourselves?
rooms.firstAction: Inspect two evidence cards, then spend a method seal.
rooms.method: Count recurrence, risk a calendar prediction, and keep the counterexample visible.
rooms.claimNote: Proposed structural reading. The room teaches the pressure test, not consensus.

evidence.title: Repeated cluster
evidence.text: The same mark returns often enough to become the first suspect.

evidence.title: Lunar spacing
evidence.text: The pattern has to risk a calendar-shaped prediction.

evidence.title: Missing twin
evidence.text: A similar line refuses the easy reading, so the case must answer back.

evidence.title: Sky story
evidence.text: Beautiful stories are cheap until the corpus pays for them.

evidence.title: Broken edge
evidence.text: Damage can look like grammar if the method gets greedy.

inspect.label: Room I - Inspect
inspect.question: Which marks deserve pressure?
inspect.prompt: Start with the object. Pick evidence that can be counted, tested, or used against the theory.
inspect.depth: Rongorongo teaches the verb: do not translate first. Build a case that can be attacked.

method.label: Room I - Method
method.question: Spend one method seal.
method.prompt: The deeper move costs a scarce method seal. Choose the method that turns recurrence into a test.
method.depth: Spencer should feel the constraint: a method card is not flavor text, it spends budget.

choices.title: Frequency count
choices.copy: Count recurrence before assigning meaning.

choices.title: Name the prettiest glyph
choices.copy: It feels like progress, but it explains before it measures.
choices.recoveryPrompt: That is a story too early. Spend the seal on recurrence first.

choices.title: Smooth the damaged line
choices.copy: The case gets cleaner by hiding the hard part.
choices.recoveryPrompt: Clean is not honest yet. Choose the method that keeps countable pressure.

test.label: Room I - Test
test.question: Risk the calendar hypothesis.
test.prompt: A calendar reading becomes serious only when it risks a next move.
test.depth: The player does the small version of the proof: if the count is lunar, the next move should carry the rhythm.

choices.recoveryPrompt: That breaks the alternation. Try the answer that makes the hypothesis answer back.

choices.label: story
choices.recoveryPrompt: A story is not a test. Choose the count.

counter.label: Room I - Counterexample
counter.question: What happens to the broken line?
counter.prompt: The good move is not the cleanest story. Keep the counterexample visible.
counter.depth: This is the first proof trace: the break stays inside the argument instead of getting swept away.

choices.title: Keep the counterexample
choices.copy: The missing twin stays in the proof, even when it hurts the story.

choices.title: Hide the counterexample
choices.copy: The pattern becomes cleaner by becoming less honest.
choices.recoveryPrompt: That is the move the game is built to punish. Keep the break visible.

initial: Make the calendar reading predict something.
Keep the counterexample inside the proof.
done: This is what Logan did at full scale: counted recurrence before translating.
This is what Logan did at full scale: treated short and long moon counts as a testable rhythm.
This is what Logan did at full scale: kept counterexamples inside the proof trace.

result.title: Calendar Room open.
result.body: You did not decode Rongorongo. You learned the opening method: pattern first, prediction second, honesty always.

rooms.name: Ledger Room
rooms.headline: Ledger or decoration?
rooms.lede: A tablet full of marks can look like ornament until accounting structure starts doing work.
rooms.firstAction: Find coverage, hierarchy, and formula pressure.
rooms.method: Make accounting structure visible before translation.
rooms.claimNote: Claim-bearing decipherment companion. Coverage and structure are separated from semantic interpretation.

evidence.title: Full sign dictionary
evidence.text: The room begins with full coverage: every unique sign receives a role or reading.

evidence.title: Susa vs periphery
evidence.text: Capital bookkeeping carries combinatorial structure; peripheral marks collapse toward labels.

evidence.title: Ledger grammar
evidence.text: A repeated administrative shape gives signs jobs before glosses get assigned.

evidence.title: M269 as LORD
evidence.text: A proposed reading matters only after the structural ledger frame is already paying rent.

evidence.title: Symbol gloss
evidence.text: A beautiful semantic bridge can outrun the accounting evidence.

inspect.label: Room II - Inspect
inspect.question: Which facts make this a ledger?
inspect.prompt: Look for the facts that constrain administration before any single sign gets translated.
inspect.depth: The template survives if the game can move from calendar recurrence to administrative structure.
inspect.guide: Pick two cards that force the marks into accounting structure.

method.label: Room II - Method
method.question: Spend one method seal.
method.prompt: Choose the method that prices a tablet as administration rather than decoration.
method.depth: A ledger reading earns confidence when formula, site, and role constrain each other.

choices.title: Fit the four-part ledger formula
choices.copy: Test whether signs keep stable administrative jobs.

choices.title: Translate the sign first
choices.copy: A gloss without the ledger is a loose story.
choices.recoveryPrompt: Not yet. Make the accounting structure carry the reading first.

choices.title: Flatten every site together
choices.copy: The hierarchy disappears, and so does the strongest constraint.
choices.recoveryPrompt: The room wants hierarchy preserved, not averaged away.

test.label: Room II - Test
test.question: Where should the complexity peak?
test.prompt: If the structure is administrative, the capital should carry richer dependency than peripheral labels.
test.depth: The move is not mystical: complex bookkeeping should demand more structure than commodity tags.

choices.label: periphery
choices.recoveryPrompt: Peripheral collapse is the contrast, not the peak.

choices.label: same
choices.recoveryPrompt: If every site behaves the same, the ledger argument loses its spine.

counter.label: Room II - Counterexample
counter.question: What about unsupported readings?
counter.prompt: The stronger move is to separate structural roles from semantic confidence.
counter.depth: The game lets the reader feel the difference between full coverage and fully settled meaning.

choices.title: Separate role from reading
choices.copy: A sign can have a stable ledger job before its gloss is final.

choices.title: Treat all glosses as equally settled
choices.copy: The dictionary gets stronger-looking by becoming less calibrated.
choices.recoveryPrompt: Keep the distinction: coverage is not the same thing as settled semantics.

initial: Test whether hierarchy predicts administrative complexity.
Separate structural role from semantic confidence.
done: This is what Logan did at full scale: made full-sign coverage auditable before translation.
This is what Logan did at full scale: used treewidth hierarchy to price administrative structure.
This is what Logan did at full scale: kept semantic confidence calibrated to structural support.

result.title: Ledger Room open.
result.body: You moved the template from sky-counting to accounting: coverage, hierarchy, formula, then calibrated reading.

rooms.name: Seal Room
rooms.headline: Language or label?
rooms.lede: A short seal can look too brief to carry language until position and graph structure start doing work.
rooms.firstAction: Choose evidence that survives the shortness of the inscriptions.
rooms.method: Separate seal repetition from sign meaning under harsh brevity.
rooms.claimNote: Structural decipherment claim. The room prices linguistic status without hiding the debate.

evidence.title: Full analyzed inventory
evidence.text: The proposed dictionary covers the analyzed signs before arguing status.

evidence.title: Treewidth above label threshold
evidence.text: The claim is that a label system should collapse lower than this.

evidence.title: Most connected sign
evidence.text: A frequent sign in a fixed position behaves like function, not ornament.

evidence.title: Seal animal first
evidence.text: The beautiful object can distract from the sign sequence.

evidence.title: Very short texts
evidence.text: Shortness is the hard objection; it has to be priced, not waved away.

inspect.label: Room III - Inspect
inspect.question: What survives brevity?
inspect.prompt: Pick evidence that still constrains a language-like system even when each seal is short.
inspect.depth: The third human room asks whether the method still works under the harshest length limit.
inspect.guide: Pick two cards that make the seals structural, not merely decorative.

method.label: Room III - Method
method.question: Spend one method seal.
method.prompt: Choose the method that tests language-like structure without pretending the debate is easy.
method.depth: The point is not to make short texts long. It is to ask what structure must be carried at once.

choices.title: Fit the positional formula
choices.copy: Test whether signs keep stable jobs inside a four-part seal shape.

choices.title: Explain the animal icon
choices.copy: It is vivid, but it is not the sign grammar yet.
choices.recoveryPrompt: The seal room is about sign sequence first. Spend the seal on position.

choices.title: Use entropy alone
choices.copy: Surprise is useful, but the room asks what structure must be carried together.
choices.recoveryPrompt: Entropy is not enough here. Use positional structure.

test.label: Room III - Test
test.question: What should a label-only system do?
test.prompt: If these were just labels, the dependency graph should collapse toward lower structure.
test.depth: This is the room's wager: treewidth prices the difference between label-like and language-like structure.

choices.label: collapses
choices.recoveryPrompt: The reported measurement is the opposite: structure holds above a label threshold.

choices.label: ignore
choices.recoveryPrompt: Brevity is the objection. The room only opens when you keep it visible.

counter.label: Room III - Counterexample
counter.question: What about the non-linguistic objection?
counter.prompt: The honest move keeps the dispute in view while testing whether structure survives it.
counter.depth: A strong public claim is not made stronger by pretending the live objection does not exist.

choices.title: Keep the debate in the trace
choices.copy: The result enters the language-vs-label debate with a measurable pressure test.

choices.title: Declare the debate settled
choices.copy: The claim sounds stronger by becoming less honest.
choices.recoveryPrompt: Keep the debate visible. The method should survive pressure, not dodge it.

initial: Test position and treewidth against label-only structure.
done: This is what Logan did at full scale: made the analyzed sign inventory explicit before interpretation.
This is what Logan did at full scale: used position and treewidth to test language-like structure.
This is what Logan did at full scale: kept the non-linguistic objection inside the public claim.

result.title: Seal Room open.
result.body: You carried the method through brevity: coverage, position, treewidth, and an honest objection.

rooms.name: Whale Room
rooms.headline: When words fail, what remains?
rooms.lede: The tutorial is over. This public gate asks whether cadence can carry signal after vocabulary collapses into noise.
rooms.firstAction: Earned room: match signal shape before claiming meaning.
rooms.method: Hear signal where terminal language first looks like noise.
rooms.claimNote: Cadence room. This teaches signal discipline, not a claim that whale speech has been decoded.

evidence.title: Call contour
evidence.text: A shape can repeat even when a word is unavailable.

evidence.title: Response timing
evidence.text: Timing can carry relation before translation is possible.

evidence.title: Noise floor
evidence.text: The room only works if noise is bounded instead of romanticized.

evidence.title: Human word
evidence.text: This may be emotionally tempting, but the room has not earned translation.

evidence.title: One beautiful call
evidence.text: One moving signal is not a structure yet.

inspect.label: Final Boss - Inspect
inspect.question: What survives without words?
inspect.prompt: Whale knowledge is earned by refusing translation until cadence earns structure.
inspect.depth: Rooms I-III taught restraint. Now the gold pulse disappears and the player has to keep the method.
inspect.guide: Pick two cadence facts that can be measured without pretending to know the words.

method.label: Final Boss - Method
method.question: Spend one method seal.
method.prompt: Choose the method that matches shape while keeping meaning sealed.
method.depth: The earned move is to compare contour and timing before semantic naming.

choices.title: Match contour and timing
choices.copy: Treat cadence shape as evidence, not as a translated sentence.

choices.title: Translate the whale
choices.copy: This is the boss trap. A claim can sound magical while losing the method.
choices.recoveryPrompt: Not earned. Match cadence shape before meaning.

choices.title: Ignore the noise floor
choices.copy: Signal gets easier only because the hard boundary vanished.
choices.recoveryPrompt: The water is part of the problem. Bound the noise first.

test.label: Final Boss - Test
test.question: Which match is earned?
test.prompt: A real cadence hypothesis should match repeated contour and response timing, not a human gloss.
test.depth: Spencer earns the whale knowledge by choosing the measurable shape over the mythic translation.
test.pattern: contour + timing / noise bounded / meaning sealed

choices.label: word
choices.recoveryPrompt: The room is won by shape, not by pretending the word is known.

choices.label: vibe
choices.recoveryPrompt: Emotion can motivate attention, but it cannot carry the proof.

counter.label: Final Boss - Counterexample
counter.question: What knowledge is actually earned?
counter.prompt: The boss opens only if you leave translation sealed and take the cadence result.
counter.depth: The reward is stronger because it is smaller: signal shape survived; translation did not get smuggled in.

choices.title: Earn cadence knowledge
choices.copy: The proof trace says what was learned: contour, timing, and bounded noise.

choices.title: Claim whale language decoded
choices.copy: That would make the ending louder and weaker.
choices.recoveryPrompt: Boss rule: take the smaller true knowledge.

initial: Keep noise and distance in the model.
Earn signal knowledge without fake translation.
done: This is what Logan did at full scale: treated dying-language noise as a measurable channel problem.
This is what Logan did at full scale: kept entropy, degeneracy, and signal decay in the same frame.
This is what Logan did at full scale: separated earned cadence knowledge from unearned translation.

result.title: Whale Cadence earned.
result.body: You reached the final boss and took the smaller true reward: cadence can preserve signal before words are recovered.
